title: How to Work with Triggers Without Avoiding Life
researcherTopic: Eastern philosophy for Western minds, the wisdom of insecurity
metaDescription: Learn to work with your trauma triggers not by avoiding them, but by understanding their language. This article explores how to recalibrate your nervous system for safety.
imageDescription: A delicate piece of pottery, repaired with shimmering gold, sits in the soft, warm light of early morning. The image evokes a sense of gentle, beautiful healing and the art of mending what was once broken.
htmlContent:
There is a quality of attention that most people never discover with how to work with triggers without avoiding life ... not because it is hidden or esoteric, but because the noise of ordinary survival drowns it out before it has a chance to register. We spend years navigating the aftermath of experiences that rewired our nervous systems, convinced that understanding alone will be enough to undo what was done. And then one morning, the understanding arrives not as a thought but as a sensation ... something the body has known all along that the mind is only now catching up to.

I have sat with hundreds of people who describe this exact territory ~ the space where how to work with triggers without avoiding life becomes not just a concept to understand but a lived reality to work through, moment by moment, breath by breath.

The Architecture of What Happened

Alan Watts's work on Eastern philosophy for Western minds, the wisdom of insecurity illuminates something that clinical language often obscures: the body is not merely affected by trauma. It becomes the primary site where trauma lives, breathes, and perpetuates itself. What we call complex trauma is not a single event but a sustained reorganization of the nervous system that occurs when safety is absent during the years when safety matters most.

The architecture of this reorganization is remarkably consistent across individuals, even as the specific experiences differ. The nervous system learns, under conditions of chronic threat, to prioritize detection over connection, vigilance over rest, performance over presence. These are not character flaws. They are survival adaptations ... brilliant ones, in fact ~ that served their purpose under impossible conditions.

Pete Walker's framework of the four F types ~ fight, flight, freeze, and fawn - offers a map of these survival adaptations that is both clinically precise and deeply human. Each type represents not a personality but a strategy, developed in childhood, for managing unbearable circumstances. The fighter learned that aggression creates distance. The fleer learned that productivity creates value. The freezer learned that disappearing creates safety. The fawner learned that compliance creates connection.

For further reading and professional resources, visit the EMDR International Association.

I want to talk about something that most people don't understand about triggers. They're not just emotional. They're physical. Your body doesn't just "feel upset" when triggered - it launches a full physiological cascade. Cortisol spikes. Heart rate increases. Blood pressure rises. Digestion shuts down. Peripheral vision narrows. Your body is preparing for a threat that isn't there anymore.

This is why "just calm down" doesn't work. You can't talk yourself out of a physiological state. It's like telling someone having an asthma attack to "just breathe normally." The system is doing what it's doing. You have to work with it, not against it.

Deb Dana's work on the polyvagal ladder gives us a map for this. When you're triggered, you've dropped down the ladder - from ventral vagal (safe, social) to sympathetic (fight/flight) or dorsal vagal (freeze/collapse). The way back up isn't through the mind. It's through the body. Through breath. Through movement. Through co-regulation with a safe other. Through tiny, repeated experiences of "I survived that. I'm still here. The danger passed."

What the Body Remembers

Peter Levine's somatic experiencing approach recognizes something that talk therapy alone often misses: the body holds completion impulses ~ movements, sounds, defensive responses ~ that were interrupted during traumatic experiences. These incomplete responses remain stored in the nervous system, creating a kind of perpetual readiness that presents as chronic tension, hypervigilance, or the strange exhaustion that comes from a body that never fully stands down.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

In my years of working in this territory, I have learned that the most genuine shifts often happen not through insight but through sensation - through the slow, careful process of allowing the body to complete what it started and was never permitted to finish. A fist that finally clenches. A voice that finally rises. A body that finally runs. These are not dramatic therapeutic moments. They are quiet completions - the nervous system finally finishing a sentence it began decades ago.

The mind is not the enemy. The identification with it is.

Stephen Porges' polyvagal theory provides the neurobiological framework for understanding why these completions matter. The autonomic nervous system operates through three primary states: the ventral vagal state of social engagement and safety, the sympathetic state of mobilization and threat response, and the dorsal vagal state of immobilization and shutdown. Complex trauma typically results in a nervous system that oscillates between sympathetic activation and dorsal collapse, rarely finding its way to the ventral vagal state where genuine connection and healing become possible.

Sit with that.

The window of tolerance ... the range of arousal within which a person can function effectively ~ narrows significantly in the aftermath of complex trauma. Stimuli that would be manageable for a regulated nervous system become overwhelming. Situations that would be boring for someone with a wide window of tolerance become activating. The result is a life lived at the extremes ... either too much or too little, rarely in the middle ground where detail and choice become available.

The Parts That Protect

Dick Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model offers another lens for understanding the internal territory of complex trauma: the recognition that what we call symptoms are actually the activities of protective parts ... internal figures that took on extreme roles during childhood to manage unbearable experiences. The inner critic is not the enemy. It is a protector that learned, under terrible conditions, that keeping you small was the safest strategy available.

Working with these parts requires something that feels counterintuitive to most people: curiosity rather than combat. The impulse to silence the critical voice, to override the anxious part, to push past the frozen part - these impulses, however understandable, replicate the very dynamic that created the problem. The parts learned their roles in an environment where their needs were dismissed. Dismissing them again, even in the name of healing, only reinforces their conviction that they must work harder.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation extends this understanding by mapping how different parts of the self can become organized around different traumatic experiences, sometimes operating with limited awareness of each other. This is not the dramatic dissociation of popular imagination. It is the quiet, everyday experience of feeling like a different person in different contexts - competent at work and collapsed at home, articulate with strangers and mute with intimates, functional in crisis and paralyzed in calm.

Most of what passes for healing is just rearranging the furniture in a burning house.

The Contemplative Dimension

There is a dimension of this work that clinical language struggles to capture ... the dimension that Jiddu Krishnamurti pointed to when he spoke of observation without the observer, and that Alan Watts illuminated when he described the wisdom of insecurity. The contemplative traditions, across cultures and centuries, have recognized something that modern trauma research is only beginning to articulate: that the deepest healing occurs not through the acquisition of new strategies but through the dissolution of the identification with the wounded self.

Here's the thing about triggers. They're not random. They feel random because the connection between the trigger and the original wound is often invisible to the conscious mind. But your amygdala knows. Your body knows. That's why you can be having a perfectly fine day and then someone says something in a certain tone of voice and suddenly you're seven years old again.

Stephen Porges calls this neuroception - the nervous system's ability to detect threat below the level of conscious awareness. It's faster than thought. It's older than language. And it's incredibly accurate - not to present reality, but to past experience. Your trigger response isn't an overreaction. It's a perfectly calibrated reaction to something that happened before. The problem is that it's happening now, when the danger is gone.

Can you think of a recent trigger? Something that set you off more than the situation warranted? Don't judge it. Just notice it. Now ask yourself - what did that moment remind your body of? Not your mind. Your body. Where did you feel it? What age did you feel? That's the thread. That's where the healing work lives.

That becomes not spiritual bypassing. It is not the premature transcendence of pain in favor of a constructed equanimity. It is something far more radical and far more grounded: the recognition that awareness itself ~ the capacity to witness experience without being consumed by it ~ is not damaged by trauma. It is obscured by trauma. The protective structures that the nervous system builds in response to overwhelming experience create layers of reactivity that make it difficult to access the awareness that was always present beneath them.

The Practice of Returning

Tara Brach's RAIN technique ... Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Nurture ~ offers a bridge between clinical trauma work and contemplative practice that many people find accessible and effective. The technique does not require a meditation cushion or a spiritual framework. It requires only the willingness to pause in the midst of activation and bring a quality of curious, compassionate attention to whatever is arising.

A simple grounding stone that fits in your pocket is Worry Stone - Amethyst (paid link).

Recognize: notice what is happening. Not the story about what is happening, but the raw sensory experience ... the tightness in the chest, the heat in the face, the impulse to flee or fight or freeze or please. Allow: let it be there without trying to fix it, change it, or make it go away. Investigate: bring gentle curiosity to the experience ... where does it live in the body? What does it need? What is it protecting? Nurture: offer whatever quality of care the experience seems to need ... perhaps a hand on the heart, perhaps a silent acknowledgment that this is hard.

I know, I know.

The simplicity of this practice belies its power. For a nervous system that has spent decades in reactive mode, the act of pausing - of creating even a few seconds of space between stimulus and response - is pioneering. It is in that gap, as Viktor Frankl noted, that freedom lives. And it is in that gap that the nervous system begins to discover something it may never have known: that it is possible to be present to difficult experience without being destroyed by it.

What the Body Knows Now

Recovery from complex trauma is not a return to a previous state. There is no previous state to return to ... the trauma occurred during the developmental period when the self was still being formed. Recovery is therefore not restoration but creation: the building of a self that was never given the conditions to emerge.

That becomes both the difficulty and what makes this work is the work. It is difficult because there is no template, no memory of wholeness to guide the process. It is beautiful because the self that emerges is not a replica of something lost but something genuinely new ~ something that carries the wisdom of survival without being defined by it.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link).

Pat Ogden's sensorimotor psychotherapy approach recognizes that this emergence happens not primarily through narrative but through the body - through the gradual expansion of the capacity to tolerate sensation, to track internal experience, to allow movement impulses to complete themselves. The body, which has been the primary site of traumatic storage, becomes the primary site of healing. Not through force or override, but through the patient, respectful process of relearning how to inhabit it.

What would it mean to stop negotiating with yourself - to stop treating your own healing as a project to be managed and instead allow it to unfold at its own pace, in its own way, guided not by the mind's agenda but by the body's wisdom?

The question is not whether you are ready. The question is whether you are willing to discover what exists on the other side of the armor you have spent a lifetime building.
